topic: FFI - CStr and CString: Null-Terminated String Safety

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to call a legacy C function from our Rust wrapper but got this error: NulError at offset 5. My code looks like it's just passing a string directly, so why is the null terminator thing breaking me?

That NulError happens because C APIs expect null-terminated strings, and your Rust string isn't automatically null-terminated by default. When you create a CString with something like CString::new("hello") it allocates memory and stores the null byte at the end, but if you try to convert that raw pointer or slice into a CStr without ensuring the zero-byte exists at the boundary, the C function reads past your actual data. The key is understanding that CStr requires a visible nul byte before you can safely hand off ownership of those bytes to unsafe C code.
So I shouldn't be using as_ptr on just any string slice? What's the right way to bridge this gap when I need zero-copy access without allocating?

Exactly, you want zero-copy from static data or known buffers. If your source is a compile-time constant like b"text\0", use CStr::from_bytes_with_nul(b"text") which creates an immutable CStr directly without touching the heap. For dynamic strings that already have a nul terminator in memory, CString::new("text") will allocate and store that nul byte internally so you can safely call to_str() later. But remember that as_ptr only gives you a raw pointer; if you hand that pointer into unsafe C code, the lifetime of your Rust string must outlive that entire C call or you'll get undefined behavior.

topic: FFI - CStr and CString: Null-Terminated String Safety

turns.content: Hey, I'm looking at the code my colleague wrote for our FFI interface and it's using a raw pointer to pass a string without checking if it ends with a null byte. He just called CStr::from_ptr on whatever buffer he got from the C side and then tried to convert it immediately into a Rust String. It compiles but I'm worried about safety since we don't know for sure that the data is valid UTF-8 or actually terminated.

That's a classic trap when working with C strings in Rust. You can never just call CStr::from_ptr on an arbitrary buffer because it will panic at runtime if the memory isn't null-terminated or contains invalid UTF-8 bytes. The safe way is to first ensure the pointer points to valid data, then use CStr::to_str to attempt conversion which will catch NulError if there's a problem, and only then wrap that in a String. If you're dealing with static compile-time strings instead of dynamic heap allocation, you can zero-copy by using CStr::from_bytes_with_nul on the byte array literal like b"text\0".

Ah okay so I need to check for NulError before wrapping it. But wait, does the C side guarantee that my pointer is valid and null-terminated? If they send me a raw buffer from an unsafe block in C, can I trust that it won't have garbage after the actual string?

Exactly. When you get a pointer from an unsafe block in C, you must assume nothing about its contents beyond what the C code guarantees. The Rust side can only do basic validation via CStr::to_str which will fail if it's not valid UTF-8 or not null-terminated. You cannot rely on the memory being clean after the string ends unless your FFI contract explicitly specifies that the caller ensures a specific termination pattern, in which case you should document that requirement clearly so everyone knows to trust that boundary.
